blurb: A dainty native onion whose flower stalk crooks at the tip so its loose umbel of pink bells nods toward the ground, alive with bees in midsummer.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
spread: Clumps slowly from offset bulbs and self-sows modestly; well-behaved
seasons: Nodding pink (sometimes white) flower clusters Jun–Aug; grassy onion-scented foliage spring–summer; dies back, dormant in winter
wildlife: Nectar for bees, butterflies and hummingbirds; seeds and bulbs taken by birds and small mammals
deer: Largely deer- and rabbit-resistant — the onion scent deters most browsers, though elk/deer graze early herbage
toxic: Edible in normal food amounts; like all onions, large quantities are toxic to dogs, cats and livestock

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — a widespread North American native good to about USDA zone 3, so it sails through Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters as a dependable perennial bulb.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant bulbs in fall or set out divisions/pot-grown plants in spring after the soil thaws. It is unfussy and takes Boulder's alkaline clay well; just give it decent drainage. In containers use a free-draining mix and a pot deep enough for the bulbs to clump; water through the growing season and let it go drier as foliage dies back. Bulbs left in the ground need no winter protection here.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade — full sun gives the sturdiest stems and best bloom; it tolerates light afternoon shade, which can help in hot, dry exposures.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable, preferring humus-rich, well-drained soil but tolerant of lean and rocky ground; it does well in neutral to alkaline soil and has a high tolerance for lime, so Boulder's clay is fine with reasonable drainage.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate. Keep new plantings watered until established, then it is drought-tolerant; even, modest moisture in the growing season gives the best flowering. Avoid soggy, poorly drained soil, which rots the bulbs.[2]
care.spacing: Space bulbs or divisions about 6–12 in apart; clumps slowly widen by offset bulbs into tidy tufts.[1][2]
care.propagation: Easiest by dividing the bulb offsets — lift dormant clumps in late summer or early fall, separate the bulblets, and replant. By seed: collect ripe black seed, sow fresh in fall or cold-stratify and sow in spring; seedlings take two to three years to reach flowering size. It also self-sows modestly where happy.[1][2]
care.bloom: June through August on the Front Range — loose, nodding umbels of pink (occasionally white) bell-shaped flowers atop the characteristically crooked stalk.[1][2]
care.feeding: Minimal. A light spring topdress of compost is ample; rich feeding is unnecessary for this lean-soil native.[2]
care.maintenance: Very low. Leave foliage to ripen and recharge the bulbs, then tidy. Deadhead before seed set if you want to limit self-sowing, or leave heads for seed and birds. Divide crowded clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows modestly into open ground near the clump and increases steadily by offsets, but it is well-behaved and not aggressive; thin or move volunteers as wanted.[1][2]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free. The same onion-family pests (onion thrips, bulb rots in wet soil) can appear but are rare in a well-drained garden; the onion scent keeps deer and rabbits away.[1][2]
care.harvest: If harvesting the bulbs or leaves for the kitchen, dig in summer and use like a mild onion or chive — but harvest ONLY from plants you are certain are Allium (see edibility caution about the deadly death-camas look-alike). Leaves can be snipped through the season; bulbs are small, so harvest sparingly to keep the clump going.[3][4]

edible.summary: The bulbs and leaves are edible and used like a mild onion or chive — but ONLY eat plants that smell strongly of onion, because the deadly death-camas grows in the same meadows and looks similar.[3][4]
edible.parts: The small bulbs and the grassy leaves are the edible parts, with a mild oniony flavor; they are eaten raw in small amounts or cooked as a seasoning, and the plant is a documented Native American food and seasoning. Use it as you would a chive or scallion.[3]
edible.preparation: Snip leaves through the growing season; dig bulbs in summer. Use raw as a mild flavoring or cook into dishes like any onion. Eat in ordinary culinary amounts — like all onions, very large quantities can upset the stomach.[3]
edible.caution: CRITICAL look-alike: deadly death-camas (Toxicoscordion / Zigadenus) grows in the same mountain meadows and grasslands and has similar grass-like leaves and a bulb that can be mistaken for a wild onion — but it contains a poisonous alkaloid in all parts at all growth stages and has caused human and livestock deaths. THE TELL: true Allium smells strongly of onion; death-camas has NO onion odor. NEVER eat any wild 'onion' bulb or leaf unless it smells clearly of onion, and be certain of your identification before harvesting. Also note that, like all onions/garlic, the plant is toxic to dogs, cats, and livestock in quantity. If poisoning is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435).[3][4]
